33.19: The full record: Manasseh’s prayer, God’s reception of it, all his sins and unfaithfulness, and the sites of his idolatry before he humbled himself. As a revelation of God, the comprehensive record that includes both the sin and the repentance establishes the divine economy: nothing is hidden, but nothing is beyond the reach of the divine response to humility. This is the theology of the final judgment as Paul describes it: “God will bring every deed into judgment” (Eccl 12:14) — but in Christ, the judgment is borne by the Son so that those who humble themselves stand forgiven.
33.20: Manasseh dies and is buried in his own house. The Chronicler records no ceremony of public honor — the ambiguity of his legacy remains. As a theme, the absence of the full burial honor for Manasseh reflects the partial and mixed nature of his reign: remarkable repentance but too much damage done. This points to the need for a king whose reign is unambiguously faithful from beginning to end — which no son of David achieves until Christ.
33.21: Amon’s two-year reign. As a theme, the brief reign of the apostate son follows the pattern of sudden truncation for those who persist in rebellion against YHWH’s covenant. The brevity of Amon’s reign is itself a theological message: the anti-Davidic king cannot sustain his position. In contrast, Christ’s kingdom “shall have no end” (Luke 1:33) because his faithfulness is unbroken.
33.22: Amon sacrificed to the same idols his father had made before his repentance. As a theme, the selective inheritance — taking the sin but not the repentance — represents the human tendency to choose the easier path. As a revelation of God, this verse establishes that repentance cannot be inherited; it must be personally appropriated. The NT equivalent: “Do not think you can say to yourselves, ‘We have Abraham as our father’” (Matt 3:9). Each person must repent and believe.
33.23: “He did not humble himself before the LORD as his father Manasseh had humbled himself, but Amon incurred more and more guilt.” The deliberate contrast with Manasseh makes Amon’s refusal the more culpable: he knew what repentance looked like and refused it. As a revelation of God, the one who has seen repentance modeled and refuses it bears greater guilt — a principle Christ articulates in the judgment of Chorazin and Bethsaida (Matt 11:21-22): greater light brings greater judgment if rejected.
33.24: Amon is assassinated by his own servants. As a theme, the king destroyed by those within his own household echoes the pattern of betrayal by intimates that culminates in Judas’s betrayal of Christ. The inside betrayal of the anointed — or the one who should have been faithful — is a recurring motif (Ps 41:9: “Even my close friend in whom I trusted, who ate my bread, has lifted his heel against me”; John 13:18).
33.25: “The people of the land struck down all who had conspired against King Amon and made his son Josiah king.” The popular vindication of the Davidic line — the common people installing the heir — is a covenant act: the people refusing to let the Davidic dynasty fall to conspiracy. As a shadow, this popular vindication of the Davidic heir anticipates the crowds who welcome Jesus into Jerusalem (Matt 21:8-9) and who will form the nucleus of the messianic people, even as the elites plot his death.

34.1: Josiah is eight years old at accession. As a type, the young king who will prove to be the last great faithful Davidic ruler before the exile is a foreshadowing of the one who, as a twelve-year-old, was already “about his Father’s business” in the temple (Luke 2:49). Both are young men whose commitment to YHWH is evident before the world expects it.
34.2: “He did what was right in the sight of the LORD, walking in the ways of his ancestor David and not turning aside to the right or to the left.” The Deuteronomic formula of perfect obedience (“not turning aside to right or left”) is used of the ideal Deuteronomic king (Deut 17:20; Josh 1:7). No Davidic king fully embodies this ideal — the formula describes what they should be. Christ perfectly embodies it: “He committed no sin, neither was deceit found in his mouth” (1 Pet 2:22; Isa 53:9).
34.3: “In the eighth year of his reign, while still young, he began to seek (dāraš) the God of his ancestor David.” As a type, the young king beginning to seek God before the visible reform starts anticipates the boy Jesus in the temple: the divine Son already occupied with his Father’s house before his public ministry (Luke 2:41-52). Both begin their decisive engagement with God’s purposes at a young age. The dāraš (seeking) that the Chronicler tracks through the Davidic kings finds its ultimate expression in Christ, who is both the seeker and the one sought (Luke 19:10: “The Son of Man came to seek and to save the lost”).
34.4: The demolition of Baal altars in Josiah’s presence — the king personally overseeing the destruction of false worship. As a type, the anointed king driving out idolatry from the covenant people’s land anticipates Christ’s temple cleansing (John 2:13-17; Matt 21:12-13): “Zeal for your house has consumed me” (John 2:17, quoting Ps 69:9). The holy zeal of the anointed king against that which defiles the Father’s house runs from Josiah to Christ.
34.5: Burning the bones of idolatrous priests on their own altars permanently defiles those altars against future use. As a type, the radical, irreversible dismantling of the cult apparatus by the faithful king points to Christ’s own irreversible act: his once-for-all sacrifice (Heb 10:12-14) permanently dismantles the need for any further sacrifice. “Where there is forgiveness of these, there is no longer any offering for sin” (Heb 10:18).
34.6: The reform extends into the northern territories. As a theme, the pan-Israelite scope of Josiah’s reform anticipates the eschatological reunion of all Israel that the prophets project: “I will take the stick of Joseph… and join it with the stick of Judah” (Ezek 37:19). Christ’s mission is explicitly pan-Israelite before it extends to the Gentiles: “I was sent only to the lost sheep of the house of Israel” (Matt 15:24), and Paul sees in Christ the “one new man in place of the two” (Eph 2:15).
34.7: The comprehensive vocabulary of destruction applied to every idol — cut down, ground to powder, demolished throughout all Israel. As a shadow, the total annihilation of the idol-cult by the faithful king prefigures Christ’s final destruction of every anti-God power: “He must reign until he has put all his enemies under his feet. The last enemy to be destroyed is death” (1 Cor 15:25-26). The comprehensiveness of Josiah’s reform is the OT shadow of the comprehensiveness of Christ’s eschatological victory.
34.8: Josiah initiates the temple repair in his eighteenth year. As a theme, the king who purges the land and then turns to repair the house of God establishes the sequence that matters: external idolatry is addressed before the internal sanctuary. As a revelation of God, this sequence points to Christ’s order of priorities: the temple must be cleansed (John 2) before the temple can become what it was meant to be — a house of prayer for all nations (Mark 11:17).
34.9: The temple-tax money gathered from Manasseh, Ephraim, all the remnant of Israel, and all Judah and Benjamin. As a theme, the pan-Israelite contribution to the temple repair represents a renewed covenant solidarity: scattered Israel gathering its resources around the house of YHWH. This anticipates the NT image of the church as a spiritual house built from living stones “from every tribe and language and people and nation” (Rev 5:9) — the true pan-Israelite temple that Christ builds.
34.10: The money entrusted to workmen who oversee the LORD’s house, specifically those “who work faithfully.” As a theme, faithful stewardship of resources entrusted for the building of God’s house is the OT instance of the parable of the talents (Matt 25:14-30): those who work faithfully with what has been entrusted are praised; the house is built by faithful labor with the Master’s resources.
34.11: “To provide for the buildings that the kings of Judah had neglected.” As a theme, the restoration of what prior unfaithfulness had allowed to decay is the work of the faithful king. Christ comes to restore what the long history of human unfaithfulness has broken: “I will restore to you the years that the swarming locust has eaten” (Joel 2:25). Every act of temple repair in the OT is a shadow of the ultimate restoration that Christ brings.
34.12: The Levites faithfully supervise the construction work. As a theme, the proper Levitical oversight of the temple economy — the priestly intermediaries ensuring the work is done rightly — is the OT form of what the NT describes as the church’s leadership: those appointed to oversee the building of God’s spiritual house (Eph 4:11-12), equipping the saints for the work of ministry so that the body is built up.
34.13: Levites serve as supervisors of burden-bearers and directors of all workers, with scribes and officials and gatekeepers also involved. As a theme, the organized administrative structure of the temple-building project reflects the divine order of the covenant community. Paul’s description of the body with many members each functioning in their role (1 Cor 12:12-27) is the NT form of this cooperative building structure under divine oversight.
34.14: “Hilkiah the priest found the book of the law of the LORD given through Moses.” As a shadow pointing toward Christ, the discovery of the lost Word of God in the temple is one of the most theologically freighted events in the OT. The Word was present all along but unrecognized, housed in the physical structure of the temple. John 1:14 — “The Word became flesh and dwelt (eskēnōsen — tabernacled) among us” — is the NT fulfillment: the Word is no longer a scroll hidden in the temple but the living Son present among his people. The book that was found in the house of God becomes the God who dwells in the house of his people.
34.15: Hilkiah’s announcement — “I have found the book of the law.” As a theme, the finding of the lost Word that transforms a kingdom resonates with the parables of the lost sheep, lost coin, and lost son (Luke 15): what was lost is found, and joy follows. The nation’s response to the found book (tearing clothes, seeking the LORD) is the appropriate response to finding the Word — the same response that genuine hearing of the gospel produces.
34.16: Shaphan brings the book to the king and reads it aloud. As a theme, the reading of the Word of God before the king — the representative of the covenant people — is the public proclamation of the law that the Deuteronomic king was supposed to read daily (Deut 17:18-19). The reading that produces transformation is what James calls being “doers of the word and not hearers only” (Jas 1:22): Josiah hears and acts. This is precisely the posture toward the Word that Christ calls his hearers to (Luke 8:21: “My mother and brothers are those who hear the word of God and do it”).
34.17: The accountable reporting of the money collected and distributed. As a theme, the transparent stewardship of resources in the service of God’s house reflects the accountability that Christ builds into his kingdom: “From everyone who has been given much, much will be required” (Luke 12:48). The accounting of resources for the temple is a shadow of the final accounting before the one who knows all stewardship.
34.18: Shaphan reads the book before the king. As a revelation of God, the moment when the Word of God is read and received by the covenant people’s representative is a salvific moment. Christ himself reads the scripture in the Nazareth synagogue and declares, “Today this scripture has been fulfilled in your hearing” (Luke 4:21): the Word of God read and the Word incarnate speaking are the same Word, now present in person.
34.19: “When the king heard the words of the law, he tore his clothes.” As a type, the king whose heart is broken by the Word of God contrasts sharply with Jehoiakim (Jer 36:23-24) who cuts the scroll and throws it in the fire. Josiah’s torn clothes are the physical enactment of the “broken and contrite heart” that God does not despise (Ps 51:17). Christ himself is moved to tears at the hardness of Jerusalem (Luke 19:41) — the opposite of Jehoiakim, the anti-Josiah.
34.20: The delegation sent to inquire of the LORD. As a theme, the king who does not presume to interpret the Word on his own but seeks prophetic guidance reflects the epistemic humility that Christ commends: “Apart from me you can do nothing” (John 15:5). Josiah has the text but seeks the living prophetic voice that interprets it. The NT equivalent is the Spirit who “will guide you into all the truth” (John 16:13).
34.21: “Great is the wrath of the LORD that is poured out on us because our fathers have not kept the word of the LORD.” Josiah’s diagnosis is precise: the accumulated debt of covenant unfaithfulness has created a judicial situation that must be addressed. As a revelation of God, this verse captures the diagnosis of the human condition that the gospel addresses: “The wrath of God is revealed from heaven against all ungodliness and unrighteousness” (Rom 1:18). Christ comes to bear this wrath on behalf of those who cannot bear it themselves.
34.22: The delegation goes to Huldah the prophetess. As a revelation of God, the fact that YHWH’s word comes through a woman prophet — when male prophets are also available — establishes the principle that prophetic authority is not determined by gender but by YHWH’s choice. In the new covenant, the Spirit is poured out on “your sons and your daughters” (Acts 2:17), removing the gender restriction on prophetic witness. Huldah is an OT anticipation of this new-covenant reality.
34.23: “Thus says the LORD, the God of Israel.” The messenger formula establishing Huldah’s prophetic authority is the same formula used by every true prophet from Moses to Malachi. As a revelation of God, the consistent use of this formula across the OT establishes the coherence of the prophetic word: one God speaking through many voices. Christ fulfills this by being not merely the messenger but the message: “In many and various ways God spoke… but in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son” (Heb 1:1-2).
34.24: “I will bring disaster upon this place and upon its inhabitants — all the curses written in the book.” The Deuteronomic curse-fulfillment mechanism: the covenantal consequences of disobedience, written in advance, are now activated. As a revelation of God, this verse establishes that divine judgment is not arbitrary but covenantal: it follows the terms announced at Sinai. Christ bears precisely these curses on behalf of his people: “Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us” (Gal 3:13).
34.25: “Because they have abandoned me and made offerings to other gods… my wrath will be poured out on this place and will not be quenched.” The unquenchable wrath corresponds to the unquenchable fire of Gehenna that Jesus uses as the image of final judgment (Mark 9:43). The wrath that will not be quenched in Jerusalem’s history (fulfilled in 586 BCE) is the OT form of what the NT presents as the eschatological wrath from which Christ saves those who believe (Rom 5:9; 1 Thess 1:10).
34.26: The second oracle — addressed to Josiah personally — distinguishes corporate judgment from individual mercy. As a revelation of God, this structural distinction in Huldah’s two-part oracle establishes a principle that Christ himself articulates: the judgment on the nation (Jerusalem will fall) and the mercy for the believing individual (those who flee to the mountains will be saved, Matt 24:16-18) are both real and must not be collapsed into each other.
34.27: “Because your heart was tender and you humbled yourself before God when you heard his words against this place and its inhabitants, and you humbled yourself before me and tore your clothes and wept before me, I have heard you.” The hearing of YHWH follows the tearing of the heart. As a revelation of God, this verse is a condensed version of the gospel: the broken and contrite heart does not go unheard. Christ himself “offered up prayers and supplications, with loud crying and tears” and “was heard because of his reverence” (Heb 5:7). The tender-hearted king who weeps before God finds YHWH listening — and so does the High Priest who weeps in Gethsemane.
34.28: “Your eyes shall not see all the disaster that I will bring upon this place.” Josiah is spared the sight of Jerusalem’s fall by a merciful death. As a type, this mercy-within-judgment echoes Simeon’s blessing: “Lord, now you let your servant depart in peace, for my eyes have seen your salvation” (Luke 2:29-30). Both are faithful covenant servants granted peace in their death before the judgment falls. The difference: Simeon has seen the salvation that Josiah only hoped for.
34.29: Josiah summons the elders of Judah and Jerusalem for the covenant renewal. As a type, the anointed king assembling the whole people before the Word of God for covenant renewal anticipates the Last Supper: Christ assembling his disciples to establish the new covenant in his blood (Matt 26:26-28). Both are the king’s covenant assembly at a pivotal moment before his own death.
34.30: The reading of the covenant book “in the hearing” of the whole assembly — men, women, children, priests, Levites. As a type, the public reading of the Word before the covenant people assembled anticipates Paul’s instructions: “When this letter has been read among you, have it also read in the church of the Laodiceans” (Col 4:16). The hearing of God’s word by the entire gathered community is the constitutive act of the covenant people from Sinai through Josiah to the church.
34.31: “The king stood in his place and made a covenant before the LORD, to follow the LORD and keep his commandments… with all his heart and all his soul.” The full Shema-love formula in a covenant-making context. As a type, the Davidic king making covenant with YHWH on behalf of the people is the pattern that Christ fulfills: he is the covenant himself (Isa 42:6: “I will give you as a covenant for the people”), the one who gives his life as the covenant-sealing sacrifice (Heb 9:15: “he is the mediator of a new covenant, so that those who are called may receive the promised eternal inheritance”).
34.32: Josiah requires all who were present to stand to the covenant. As a theme, the corporate binding to the covenant by the faithful king on behalf of the whole people anticipates the work of Christ as the one who establishes the new covenant obligation on behalf of all who are in him. In Christ, the covenant is no longer merely imposed from outside but fulfilled from within by the one who “through the eternal Spirit offered himself without blemish to God” (Heb 9:14).
34.33: “Josiah removed all the abominations from all the territories belonging to the people of Israel and required all who were present in Israel to serve the LORD their God. All his days they did not turn aside from following the LORD.” The Chronicler’s closing evaluation of Josiah is unqualified: under this king, the covenant was fully kept. This is the highest point of the Davidic dynasty before its fall. As a type, Josiah’s reign of total covenant faithfulness is the OT’s closest anticipation of what Christ permanently establishes: the eternal kingdom in which the people of God “will serve him day and night in his temple” (Rev 7:15), never again turning aside from following the LORD their God.

35.1: A direct connection. Josiah keeps the Passover on the fourteenth day of the first month — the precise date of Exod 12:6 (the original Passover) and Num 9:3 (the standardized form). Unlike Hezekiah’s Passover kept in the second month (2 Chr 30:2-3, necessitated by Levitical uncleanness), Josiah’s is metronomically exact. The calendar precision is the Chronicler’s signal that the covenant is being kept exactly as given. Christ the Passover Lamb (1 Cor 5:7) was crucified on the fourteenth of Nisan (John 19:14,31,42) — the same date Josiah inaugurated. The Passover kept on the day reaches its fulfillment in the sacrifice offered on the day.
35.2: A shadow. Josiah stationing the priests in their divisions — wayya‘amîdēm ‘al-miškĕmôtām, ‘he caused them to stand upon their stations’ — is the royal-priestly coordination that the Passover requires. The king who ensures the priests are positioned mirrors the pattern of Moses (Exod 40:12-15) and Solomon (2 Chr 8:14). In the new covenant, Christ both positions and is the priest: ‘he always lives to make intercession’ (Heb 7:25), the high priest perpetually at his station before the Father. The encouragement Josiah gives the priests (wayhazzeqêm, ‘he strengthened them’) anticipates Christ who equips his priestly people (Eph 4:11-12).
35.3: A shadow. Josiah’s instruction to the Levites — ‘Put the holy ark in the house that Solomon son of David, king of Israel, built. You need not carry it on your shoulders’ — releases the Levites from the transportation burden and redirects their service to worship and preparation. The ark at rest is the typological precedent for Hebrews’ argument: now that the high priest has entered the true sanctuary (Heb 9:11-12), the preparatory carrying work is complete. The Levites can now serve the people in preparation rather than transport. Christ, having carried the burden of atonement to its destination, releases his servants for ministry rather than the carrying of sacred burdens.
35.4: A shadow. ‘Prepare yourselves according to your ancestral families and divisions, as prescribed in the writing of David king of Israel and as written in the document of Solomon his son’ — the coordinated preparation that requires both David’s organizational blueprint and Solomon’s written instructions. The Passover is prepared according to written authority, not improvised. The written authority behind the Passover points to the written word that governs the new covenant Passover: Christ’s command at the Last Supper was immediately transmitted as written apostolic tradition (1 Cor 11:23: ‘I received from the Lord what I also delivered to you’). The supper is observed according to writing.
35.5: A shadow. ‘Stand in the Holy Place according to the groupings of the ancestral families of your brothers the lay people, and according to the Levitical division of a family’ — the ordered positioning of priests between the lay people and the sanctuary is the priestly mediation structure of the Passover. Every lay Israelite approaches through a priest; every priest stands at the boundary of the Holy Place. The mediation structure that positions the priest between the people and the divine presence is the type Christ fulfills: ‘there is one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus’ (1 Tim 2:5). The Levitical positioning is the structural type of the singular mediation of Christ.
35.6: A direct connection. ‘Slaughter the Passover offerings, consecrate yourselves, and prepare your brothers so that they may do according to the word of the LORD by Moses’ — the three-part commission (slaughter, consecrate, prepare) mirrors the three movements of the gospel: sacrifice (Christ’s death), sanctification (the Spirit’s work), and preparation of the church (apostolic teaching). The command to prepare brothers echoes Deut 16:1-8; the ‘word of the LORD by Moses’ is the authority structure the Passover operates within. Paul applies the same three movements in 1 Cor 5:7-8: Christ as sacrifice, the church as the unleavened community, the Passover kept in sincerity and truth.
35.7: A type. Josiah’s personal contribution — 30,000 lambs and young goats for Passover offerings, plus 3,000 cattle — is the royal gift that makes the Passover possible for the people who cannot provide for themselves. The king provides what the lay people need for the covenant meal. Christ provides what no human could supply for the true Passover: ‘Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed’ (1 Cor 5:7). The Passover lamb is not the worshipper’s contribution but the gift of the one who ensures the feast can occur. Josiah’s 30,000 lambs is a vast royal provision that is entirely superseded by the one Lamb who was slain (Rev 5:12).
35.8: A shadow. The voluntary contributions of Josiah’s officials — Hilkiah, Zechariah, and Jehiel giving 2,600 lambs and 300 cattle — is the willing generosity of the priestly community that supplements the royal provision. The leaders who give willingly out of their own resources for the community feast mirror the apostolic generosity in Acts 4:34-35 (those with resources selling and distributing) and 2 Cor 8:2-3 (the Macedonian churches giving ‘beyond their ability’ for others). The Passover feast that depends on the generosity of leaders pointing toward the community that gathers at Christ’s table because those entrusted with resources have given for the benefit of all.
35.9: A shadow. The additional Levitical contributions — 5,000 Passover offerings and 500 cattle — represent the Levites as community whose leaders also contribute. The layered generosity (king → officials → Levites) mirrors the NT pattern of giving: from the highest (the apostolic foundation) through the leadership to the whole community. The cumulative total of contributions for one Passover — thousands of animals from multiple community sectors — is the shadow of the one perfect sacrifice that Christ makes once for all (Heb 9:26-28: ‘to put away sin by the sacrifice of himself...once for all’), rendering all subsequent animal-sacrifice contributions permanently unnecessary.
35.10: A shadow. ‘When the service had been prepared, the priests stood in their positions and the Levites in their divisions, according to the king’s command’ — the entire liturgical assembly organized and ready at the king’s command is the Passover as a coordinated act of obedience. The Passover does not happen spontaneously; it is organized, commanded, and executed. The new covenant gathering for the Lord’s Supper follows the same pattern: Paul’s instruction to the Corinthians (1 Cor 11:17-34) is explicitly about how it must be ‘prepared’ — the disorder he condemns is a failure of the coordination that Josiah’s Passover models perfectly.
35.11: A direct connection. ‘They slaughtered the Passover lambs, and the priests sprinkled the blood received from the Levites’ — the blood-reception and blood-sprinkling is the central act of the Passover rite. The Levites slaughter; the priests receive the blood; the priests sprinkle. This three-step mediation of the sacrificial blood is the type of Christ’s atonement: Christ is both the lamb slaughtered and the high priest who presents the blood (Heb 9:12: ‘he entered once for all into the holy places, not by means of the blood of goats and calves but by means of his own blood, thus securing an eternal redemption’). In Christ, the slaughterer, the blood-bearer, and the sprinkler are one person — the mediatory chain collapsed into the one Mediator.
35.12: A shadow. The burnt offerings set aside for distribution ‘to give them to the groupings of the ancestral families of the lay people to offer to the LORD, as written in the Book of Moses’ — the exact distribution of the sacrifice according to written prescription ensures every family group participates. No family is left out; every household has access to the sacrifice through the organized distribution system. In Christ, the distribution of the sacrifice is universal: ‘the grace of God has appeared, bringing salvation for all people’ (Tit 2:11). The Passover distributed to every family group is the type of the gospel offered to every people group (Rev 5:9: ‘from every tribe and language and people and nation’).
35.13: A direct connection. ‘They roasted the Passover lambs over the fire as prescribed’ — the roasting method specified in Exod 12:8-9 (‘roasted over the fire, with its head, legs, and inner parts’; ‘do not eat it raw or boiled in water’) is followed exactly. The bāʾēš (in/by fire) method is the prescribed form. The fire-roasting of the Passover lamb anticipates the cross: the lamb that bore the judgment represented by fire (fire as divine judgment throughout the OT: Sodom, the incense of Nadab and Abihu, the test of Elijah). John 19:28-37 reads the crucifixion through Passover lenses: the unbroken bones (Exod 12:46; Num 9:12) fulfilled (John 19:36). The lamb roasted exactly as prescribed is the type of the death that happened exactly as written (Luke 24:44-46).
35.14: A shadow. ‘Afterward they prepared for themselves and for the priests, because the priests, the sons of Aaron, were occupied with offering the burnt offerings and the fat portions until night’ — the Levites serve the priests who are too occupied with the sacrificial ministry to prepare their own portions. The servant-of-the-servant principle: the Levites serve the priests so the priests can serve the people so the people can approach YHWH. The inverted service hierarchy (the lesser serves the greater so the greater can serve the even greater who serve the greatest) is the kingdom pattern that Christ names: ‘whoever would be great among you must be your servant, and whoever would be first among you must be slave of all’ (Mark 10:43-44).
35.15: A shadow. The singers of Asaph in their assigned places ‘according to the command of David, Asaph, Heman, and Jeduthun the king’s seer’ — the Passover is accompanied by prescribed music as the Levitical gatekeepers guard every gate. The Passover feast has a liturgical music tradition; it does not take place in silence. At the Last Supper, Jesus and the disciples ‘sang a hymn’ before going to the Mount of Olives (Matt 26:30) — almost certainly Pss 114-118, the Hallel, which the Passover seder required. The singers at their stations for Josiah’s Passover and the hymn at the end of the Last Supper are the Passover’s musical frame, pointing to the praise that surrounds the sacrifice of the true Lamb.
35.16: A shadow. ‘So all the service of the LORD was prepared that day for keeping the Passover and offering burnt offerings on the altar of the LORD, according to the command of King Josiah’ — the completeness formula that signals the Passover has been kept exactly as commanded. The threefold emphasis: all the service, that day, according to command. The precision matters. Paul’s formula for the Lord’s Supper closes with the same completeness concern: ‘as often as you eat this bread and drink the cup, you proclaim the Lord’s death until he comes’ (1 Cor 11:26). The ‘until he comes’ is the timeline of the new covenant Passover — kept precisely, repeatedly, until the return of the one who instituted it.
35.17: A shadow. ‘The Israelites who were present kept the Passover at that time, and the Feast of Unleavened Bread for seven days’ — the coupling of Passover with the seven-day Feast of Unleavened Bread is the full liturgical unit (Exod 12:14-20; 13:3-10). The one night of the Passover lamb opens into a week of unleavened bread — the day of sacrifice extends into a season of purification. Paul applies this to the church: ‘let us therefore celebrate the festival, not with the old leaven... but with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth’ (1 Cor 5:8). The seven-day season of the church’s life is to be lived as the continuation of the Passover — the sacrifice applied to daily existence.
35.18: A direct connection. ‘No Passover like this had been kept in Israel since the days of Samuel the prophet. None of the kings of Israel had kept such a Passover as was kept by Josiah’ — the climactic Passover of the entire Davidic era; nothing like it since the tribal period before the monarchy. This is the peak of Passover observance in the OT — the most complete, most exactly prescribed, most broadly participated feast the covenant people ever kept. It is the final great OT Passover before the exile. The Last Supper is its fulfillment: ‘this is my body... this cup is the new covenant in my blood’ (Luke 22:19-20). The greatest Passover of the old covenant (Josiah’s) gives way to the one feast that renders all future Passover lamb-slaughter unnecessary.
35.19: A shadow. ‘This Passover was kept in the eighteenth year of Josiah’s reign’ — the dating formula anchors the greatest Passover in the Davidic chronology. The eighteenth year is the year of the Book of the Law’s discovery (2 Chr 34:8) — the Passover grows directly out of the rediscovery of the covenant text. The connection between the found law and the Passover celebration is the pattern of covenant renewal: hear the word → repent → keep the feast. Jesus quotes Scripture throughout his final Passover night (John 13-17 is saturated with OT allusion), and the institution of the Lord’s Supper is itself a scriptural interpretation of the Exodus event. The word precedes the feast.
35.20: A shadow. ‘After all this, when Josiah had put the temple in order, Neco king of Egypt came up to fight at Carchemish on the Euphrates, and Josiah went out to meet him’ — the abrupt transition from the greatest Passover to the king’s last battle is one of the Bible’s most jarring narrative moves. The reforming king who kept the perfect Passover marches to his death. The juxtaposition is the shadow of the Last Supper’s own narrative transition: from the Passover meal (Luke 22:14-23) to Gethsemane (Luke 22:39-46) to the arrest. The greatest feast precedes the greatest death in both cases. The Passover and the cross are inseparable.
35.21: A revelation of God. Neco’s message to Josiah — ‘I am not coming against you today but against the house with which I am at war. God has commanded me to hurry. Stop opposing God, who is with me, so he will not destroy you’ — is the Egyptian pagan king carrying a genuine divine message that Josiah fails to heed. YHWH’s word comes through an unexpected instrument: the enemy king on his way to Carchemish. The same pattern recurs throughout Scripture: Balaam’s donkey, Cyrus as YHWH’s shepherd (Isa 44:28), Nebuchadnezzar as YHWH’s servant (Jer 25:9), and ultimately — the revelation of God speaking through the Gentile instrument that Jesus identifies in Matt 12:42 (the Queen of the South) and Luke 11:31. The word of God is not confined to Israel’s official prophets.
35.22: A shadow. ‘Nevertheless Josiah did not turn away from him but disguised himself to fight with him. He did not listen to the words of Neco from the mouth of God’ — Josiah’s fatal mistake: the same disguise strategy as Ahab (2 Chr 18:29), and the same failure to receive divine communication through an unexpected channel. The parallels between Josiah and Ahab are deliberate: both disguised themselves for a battle they should not have fought; both died from archers’ arrows in that battle. The righteous king who makes one fatal blunder shadows the human need for a king who will ‘not rely on his own understanding’ (Prov 3:5). The one king who never failed to hear the Father — even when the message came through unexpected channels — is Christ (John 5:19: ‘the Son can do nothing of his own accord, but only what he sees the Father doing’).
35.23: A shadow. ‘The archers shot King Josiah, and the king said to his servants, “Take me away, for I am badly wounded”’ — the arrow that struck Josiah mirrors the arrow that killed Ahab (2 Chr 18:33-34: a randomly-aimed arrow that found its target). But with a crucial difference: Ahab’s death was YHWH’s judgment on the wicked; Josiah’s death is the suffering of the righteous king who made one fatal error of judgment. The righteous king who suffers mortal wounds in a battle he misjudged is the type of Christ, who — in an entirely different register — voluntarily entered the arena of judgment and bore wounds he did not deserve. The wounding of the king at the moment of his greatest strength is a shadow of the suffering of the innocent.
35.24: A shadow. Josiah’s servants transferring him from his war chariot to his second chariot and bringing him to Jerusalem, where he died — and the whole of Judah and Jerusalem mourning for him — is the transport of the dying king back to the royal city. The king who cannot save himself, who must be carried by others, who dies in the city of YHWH’s house — this is the shadow of the one who was taken down from the cross and buried in Jerusalem. Joseph of Arimathea’s transport of Jesus’ body (John 19:38-42) is the same movement: from the place of death, carried by servants, to a burial in the holy city. Josiah’s tomb and Jesus’ tomb are both in Jerusalem; one opened permanently on the third day.
35.25: A direct connection. ‘Jeremiah lamented for Josiah, and all the male and female singers have spoken of Josiah in their laments to this day’ — the prophetic lament for the fallen righteous king becomes a liturgical tradition that endures across generations. The lament for the lost king is one of the OT’s most persistent prophetic forms. Zechariah 12:10-11 explicitly draws on the Megiddo mourning (‘as one mourns for an only son... as one mourns over the firstborn... as the mourning of Hadad-rimmon in the plain of Megiddo’) as the type of the mourning that will occur when Israel looks on ‘him whom they have pierced.’ The lament for Josiah becomes the prophetic template for the lament at the piercing of Christ — the mourning that will flow when the ultimate Shepherd-King is recognized.
35.26: A shadow. ‘The rest of the acts of Josiah and his faithful deeds, as written in the law of the LORD’ — the standard closing formula, but with a distinctive addition: his ḥasādāyw (‘his acts of faithful covenant loyalty’) as written in the law of the LORD. Josiah’s acts of covenant loyalty are evaluated against the written Torah — his faithfulness is measured by the written word. The standard Christ sets for judgment is himself (John 12:48: ‘the word that I have spoken will judge him on the last day’) and the fulfillment of the written word (Matt 5:17: ‘do not think that I have come to abolish the Law or the Prophets; I have not come to abolish them but to fulfill them’). The written law is the standard; Christ is its fulfillment.
35.27: A shadow. ‘His acts, first and last, are recorded in the Book of the Kings of Israel and Judah’ — the final closing formula for Josiah; after him come four failed kings in rapid succession (ch 36) before the exile. Josiah is the last righteous king; his record is closed. The pattern of the Chronicles is: the righteous king whose work is complete, followed by the collapse. Christ is the one whose works are recorded not in Chronicles but in the Gospels — and John 21:25 says the record is necessarily selective: ‘were every one of them to be written, I suppose that the world itself could not contain the books.’ The chronicler closes with Josiah; the evangelist opens with the one whose acts exceed all record.
